Given some categorized constants in the:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% For each client, match with exactly one masseuse and one price.
{match(Client, Masseuse, Price): masseuse(Masseuse), price(Price)}=1 :- clients(Client).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
diplomats("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(4; 5; 9; 10).

% For each diplomat D, assign exactly 1 month M and 1 duration Du.
{assign(D, M, Du): months(M), duration(Du)}=1 :- diplomats(D).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").

% Define the relation: rocket R is developed by company C and launches in month M.
% Each rocket is developed by exactly one company and launches in exactly one month.
{develops(R, C, M): companies(C), months(M)}=1 :- names(R).
{develops(R, C, M): months(M), companies(C)}=1 :- names(R).

% Clue 1: The Worul, the rocket that will launch in February, and the rocket that will launch in January are all different rockets.
% So Worul does not launch in January (1) or February (2).
:- develops("Worul", _, 1).
:- develops("Worul", _, 2).

% Clue 2: The Dreadco is made by Ubersplore.
:- develops("Dreadco", C, M), C != "Ubersplore".

% Clue 3: The rocket developed by Permias will launch 1 month before the Foltron.
% So if Permias develops rocket P and launches in month M, then Foltron launches in month M+1.
% Also, both must be valid months (1 to 4).
:- develops(P, "Permias", M), develops("Foltron", _, M1), M1 != M + 1.

% Clue 4: The rocket that will launch in January is made by Techtrin.
:- develops(R, C, 1), C != "Techtrin".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
games("Angry Ants"; "Bubble Bombs"; "Flowcarts"; "Peter Parka"; "Wizard World").
months(3; 4; 5; 6; 7).
developers("Apptastic"; "Digibits"; "Gadingo"; "Novio"; "Vortia").

% Define the main predicate: released(G, M, D) means game G was released in month M by developer D.
released(G, M, D) :- games(G), months(M), developers(D).

% Each game is released in exactly one month by exactly one developer.
{released(G, M, D) : months(M), developers(D)} = 1 :- games(G).
{released(G, M, D) : games(G), developers(D)} = 1 :- months(M).
{released(G, M, D) : games(G), months(M)} = 1 :- developers(D).

% Clue 1: The app released by Vortia was released 2 months later than Flowcarts.
:- released("Flowcarts", M1, _), released(_, M2, "Vortia"), M2 != M1 + 2.

% Clue 2: Angry Ants was released 3 months later than the app released by Gadingo.
:- released(_, M1, "Gadingo"), released("Angry Ants", M2, _), M2 != M1 + 3.

% Clue 3: The game released by Apptastic was released 3 months later than Flowcarts.
:- released("Flowcarts", M1, _), released(_, M2, "Apptastic"), M2 != M1 + 3.
```

```asp
% Ensure each wine is assigned exactly one vintage and one type.
{assign(W, V, T) : vintage(V), type(T)} = 1 :- wine(W).

% Ensure each vintage is used exactly once.
{assign(W, V, T) : wine(W), type(T)} = 1 :- vintage(V).

% Ensure each type is used exactly once.
{assign(W, V, T) : wine(W), vintage(V)} = 1 :- type(T).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```

```asp
rankings(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Define the assignment predicate: each team is assigned one color and one ranking.
% This captures the relation among the three categories.
assign(T, R, C) :- teams(T), rankings(R), colors(C).

% Each team has exactly one ranking and one color.
{assign(T, R, C) : rankings(R), colors(C)} = 1 :- teams(T).

% Each ranking is used exactly once.
{assign(T, R, C) : teams(T), colors(C)} = 1 :- rankings(R).

{assign(T, R, C) : teams(T), rankings(R)} = 1 :- colors(C).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
superheroes("Deep Shadow"; "Green Avenger"; "Max Fusion"; "Ultra Hex").
years(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
real_names("Arnold Ashley"; "Lyle Lucas"; "Orel Osborne"; "Red Reilly").

% Each superhero is associated with exactly one real name and one year.
{assign(S, R, Y): real_names(R), years(Y)}=1 :- superheroes(S).
{assign(R, S, Y): superheroes(S), years(Y)}=1 :- real_names(R).
{assign(Y, R, S): real_names(R), superheroes(S)}=1 :- years(Y).

% Clue 1: The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, and Lyle Lucas.
% This implies that the set of individuals is: 2007 starter, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas.
% So we must have:
%   - The 2007 starter is one of the four, and all four are distinct.
%   - Therefore, the 2007 starter is not Arnold Ashley (since Arnold Ashley is not among the four listed).
%   - So Arnold Ashley ≠ 2007 starter → so Arnold Ashley started in 2009 (from clue 3).

% Clue 2: Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
% So: year(Arnold Ashley) = year(Ultra Hex) + 1
% Clue 3: The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
% So: year(Arnold Ashley) = 2009 → thus year(Ultra Hex) = 2008

% Clue 4: Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.
% So: Green Avenger → Orel Osborne

% Enforce the constraints via ASP.
:- assign("Ultra Hex", _, Y), Y != 2008.
:- assign("Arnold Ashley", _, Y), Y != 2009.
:- assign("Arnold Ashley", R, Y), R != "Arnold Ashley".
:- assign("Green Avenger", R, Y), R != "Orel Osborne".

% The 2007 starter must be one of: Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas
% But Arnold Ashley is not the 2007 starter → so Arnold Ashley ≠ 2007 starter
% So: assign("Arnold Ashley", _, 2007) is false.
:- assign("Arnold Ashley", _, 2007).

% The four people are: 2007 starter, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas
% So: Arnold Ashley is not among them → so Arnold Ashley must be Max Fusion
% So: assign("Max Fusion", "Arnold Ashley", 2009) must be true
% But we already have Arnold Ashley → 2009, so Max Fusion must be Arnold Ashley
% So: assign("Max Fusion", "Arnold Ashley", 2009) is required.

% We don't need to explicitly assert this, but we can use it to guide the search space.

% Generate all possible assignments satisfying the constraints.
% We use a single predicate assign(S, R, Y) to represent the mapping.
% The search space is generated by the choice rules and constraints above.

% The minimal set of predicates is the assignment of each superhero to a real name and year.
% The rules above define the search space and enforce the constraints.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
rankings(1; 2; 3; 4).
teams("Color Blinds"; "Night Ninjas"; "Oil Crew"; "Target Bombs").
colors("green"; "orange"; "purple"; "white").

% Each team is matched to exactly one ranking, one color.
% Use a single predicate to capture the full assignment across all categories.
% This minimizes the number of predicates to 1, while ensuring all categories are used.
match(T, C, R) :- teams(T), colors(C), rankings(R).

% Each team has exactly one color and one ranking.
{match(T, C, R): colors(C), rankings(R)}=1 :- teams(T).

% Each color is used by exactly one team.
{match(T, C, R): teams(T), rankings(R)}=1 :- colors(C).

% Each ranking is used by exactly one team.
{match(T, C, R): teams(T), colors(C)}=1 :- rankings(R).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
islands("Fushil"; "Jujihm"; "Nuhirk"; "Verinya").
years(1754; 1761; 1768; 1775).
cultures("Hakili"; "Manika'i"; "Kukani"; "Wainani").

% Define the relation: discover(I, Y, C) means island I was discovered in year Y and had culture C.
discover(I, Y, C) :- islands(I), years(Y), cultures(C).

% Each island is discovered in exactly one year and has exactly one culture.
{discover(I, Y, C) : years(Y), cultures(C)} = 1 :- islands(I).
{discover(I, Y, C) : islands(I), cultures(C)} = 1 :- years(Y).
{discover(I, Y, C) : islands(I), years(Y)} = 1 :- cultures(C).

% Clue 1: Jujihm was discovered in 1768.
:- not discover("Jujihm", 1768, _).

% Clue 2: Verinya was discovered in 1761.
:- not discover("Verinya", 1761, _).

% Clue 3: Jujihm was discovered 14 years after the island on which the Wainani people lived.
% So Wainani island was discovered in 1754.
:- not discover(I, 1754, "Wainani") :- islands(I), cultures("Wainani").

% Clue 4: The island on which the Kukani people lived was discovered 7 years after Fushil.
% So if Fushil was discovered in Y, then Kukani island was discovered in Y+7.
:- not (discover("Fushil", Y, _) ∧ discover(I, Y+7, "Kukani")) :- years(Y), islands(I).

% Clue 5: The island discovered in 1768 was either the island on which the Hakili people lived or Fushil.
% So if discover(I, 1768, C), then C = "Hakili" or I = "Fushil".
:- discover(I, 1768, C), C != "Hakili", I != "Fushil".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
companies("Banion"; "Dayero"; "Honwa"; "Torvia").
price(550; 575; 600; 625).
camera("DM-5000"; "FC-520"; "MX-827"; "Zenix 2C").

% Define the main relation: each camera is made by one company and has one price.
% This predicate captures the core matching across all three categories.
match(M, C, P) :- camera(M), companies(C), price(P).

% Each camera is matched to exactly one company and one price.
% Each company makes exactly one camera.
% Each price is assigned to exactly one camera.
% These ensure a bijection across all categories.
:- #count{C, P : match(M, C, P)} != 1, camera(M).
:- #count{M, P : match(M, C, P)} != 1, companies(C).
:- #count{M, C : match(M, C, P)} != 1, price(P).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
countries("Dominica"; "Jordan"; "Venezuela"; "Zimbabwe").
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).
silver_medals(2; 5; 6; 8).

% Define the core predicate: medals(C, G, S) means country C won G gold and S silver medals.
medals(C, G, S) :- countries(C), gold_medals(G), silver_medals(S).

% Each country has exactly one gold and one silver medal count.
{medals(C, G, S) : gold_medals(G), silver_medals(S)} = 1 :- countries(C).

% Each gold medal count is used exactly once.
{medals(C, G, S) : countries(C), silver_medals(S)} = 1 :- gold_medals(G).

% Each silver medal count is used exactly once.
{medals(C, G, S) : countries(C), gold_medals(G)} = 1 :- silver_medals(S).

% Clue 1: Venezuela won 4 gold medals.
:- not medals("Venezuela", 4, S) : silver_medals(S).

% Clue 2: The team with 2 gold medals won 6 silver medals.
:- not medals(C, 2, 6) : countries(C).

% Clue 3: The four teams are Zimbabwe, the team with 3 gold, the team with 6 silver, and the team with 8 silver.
% This implies that the country with 3 gold is not Zimbabwe unless it is, but all four are distinct.
% We enforce that the set of countries is exactly these four descriptions.
% So: the team with 3 gold must be one of the countries, and similarly for 6 and 8 silver.
% Since all are unique, we can use the uniqueness constraints above to ensure coverage.

% Clue 4: Dominica has 1 fewer gold than the team with 5 silver medals.
:- medals("Dominica", G_d, S_d), medals(X, G_x, 5), G_d != G_x - 1.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
```
```asp
borrower("Cory"; "Rosa"; "Sherrie"; "Vicki").
due_date(1; 8; 15; 22).
title("Dancing Well"; "Heaven's Seal"; "Stars Below"; "Time to Burn").

% Define the single predicate to represent the assignment: book(B, D, T)
% meaning: the book with title T was borrowed by B and is due on D.
book(B, D, T) :- borrower(B), due_date(D), title(T).

% Each borrower, due_date, and title appears exactly once.
{book(B, D, T) : due_date(D), title(T)} = 1 :- borrower(B).
{book(B, D, T) : borrower(B), title(T)} = 1 :- due_date(D).
{book(B, D, T) : borrower(B), due_date(D)} = 1 :- title(T).

% Clue 2: "Heaven's Seal is either Vicki's book or the book due on September 8."
:- not book(Vicki, D, "Heaven's Seal"), not book(B, 8, "Heaven's Seal").

% Clue 3: "Of Heaven's Seal and the title due on September 22, one was taken out by Cory and the other by Vicki."
% This means: either (Heaven's Seal → Cory, due 22 → Vicki) OR (Heaven's Seal → Vicki, due 22 → Cory)
% We can express this as:
%   (book(Cory, _, "Heaven's Seal") ∧ book(Vicki, 22, _)) ∨ (book(Vicki, _, "Heaven's Seal") ∧ book(Cory, 22, _))
% But in ASP, we use constraints to enforce that at least one of the two cases holds.
% So we add a rule that ensures this disjunction is satisfied.
:- not (book(Cory, D1, "Heaven's Seal") ∧ book(Vicki, 22, T1)), not (book(Vicki, D2, "Heaven's Seal") ∧ book(Cory, 22, T2)).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
companies("Buralde"; "Denlend"; "Irycia"; "Kimsight").
pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
themes("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

% For each company C, it matches with exactly 1 piece P and 1 theme T.
{puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), themes(T)}=1 :- companies(C).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
height(144; 147; 150; 153).
tree("Evan's Fir"; "Old Jarvis"; "Nolan's Pine"; "Zeke's Spruce").
age(79; 80; 96; 99).

% For each tree, it has exactly one height and one age.
{tree_info(Tree, Height, Age): height(Height), age(Age)}=1 :- tree(Tree).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
owners("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
years(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breeds("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

% Each owner, year, and breed appears exactly once in the assignment.
% The central predicate: win(Owner, Year, Breed) — owner's dog won in that year and is of that breed.
% We ensure one-to-one mapping across all categories.
{win(O, Y, B) : breeds(B)} = 1 :- owners(O).
{win(O, Y, B) : owners(O)} = 1 :- years(Y).
{win(O, Y, B) : years(Y)} = 1 :- breeds(B).

% Clue 1: The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
% There exists a year Y1 such that Fernando's dog won in Y1, and some dog (not necessarily Fernando's) won the dalmatian in Y1+1.
:- win(Fernando, Y1, _), win(_, Y2, "dalmatian"), Y2 = Y1 + 1.

% Clue 2: Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
% Ginger's dog won in Y1, and the irish setter won in Y2, where Y2 = Y1 + 1.
:- win(Ginger, Y1, _), win(_, Y2, "irish setter"), Y2 = Y1 + 1.

% Clue 3: Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
% Ginger's dog is of breed bulldog.
:- win(Ginger, Y, B), B != "bulldog".

% Clue 4: The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog, and Elsie's dog.
% This means the set {2006 winner, dalmatian, bulldog, Elsie's dog} contains exactly four distinct dogs.
% Since there are only four dogs, this implies that these four descriptions are all different.
% So, we enforce that:
% - The 2006 winner is not the dalmatian unless they are the same dog, but we must have four distinct entities.
% But since we are modeling one-to-one assignment, we can express this by ensuring that:
% The 2006 winner, the dalmatian, the bulldog, and Elsie's dog are all distinct in their roles.
% However, the only way to enforce this is via the uniqueness of the assignment.
% But actually, the clue is redundant if we already enforce one-to-one across all categories — it's just emphasizing that all four are present.
% So we don't need to add a constraint beyond the one-to-one assignment — the clue is satisfied by the structure.
% However, to be explicit: we can assert that the four entities are distinct, but since each dog is uniquely identified by (O,Y,B), and we have 4 owners, 4 years, 4 breeds, and the assignment is bijective, this clue is automatically satisfied.

% Therefore, no additional constraint is needed beyond the one-to-one mapping and the three specific clues.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employees("Andy"; "Brett"; "Victor"; "Zachary").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% For each employee E, there is exactly one assignment to one section S and one rider count R.
{work_assignment(E, S, R): section(S), riders(R)}=1 :- employees(E).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Charles"; "Evan"; "Karl"; "Vincent").
games(8; 9; 10; 11).
positions("center field"; "first base"; "shortstop"; "third base").

% For each player, they play in exactly one position and have exactly one number of games.
{plays(Player, Position, Games): positions(Position), games(Games)}=1 :- players(Player).
```
